perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding the provisions of this Agreement, the obligations and restrictions set forth herein regarding Confidential Information shall not apply to information that the Receiving Party can establish (i) is or becomes publicly available other than as a result of a breach of this Agreement by the Receiving Party, (ii) is lawfully received from a third party which is, to the reasonable knowledge and belief of the Receiving Party, not under any obligation of confidentiality for the benefit of the Disclosing Party, (iii) was either in the possession of or known to the Receiving Party at the time of disclosure without any limitation on use or disclosure for the benefit of the Receiving Party, or (iv) is independently developed by the Receiving Party without the use, r eference to or benefit of the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding the provisions of this Agreement, the obligations and restrictions set forth herein regarding Confidential Data shall not apply to information that the Receiving Party can establish (i) is or becomes publicly available other than as a result of a breach of this Agreement by the Receiving Party, (ii) is lawfully received from a third party which is, to the reasonable knowledge and belief of the Receiving Party, not under any obligation of confidentiality for the benefit of the Disclosing Party, (iii) was either in the possession of or known to the Receiving Party at the time of disclosure without any limitation on use or disclosure for the benefit of the Receiving Party, or (iv) is independently developed by the Receiving Party without the use, r eference to or benefit of the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Data.
perturbation.explanation: Using the term 'Confidential Data' in section 3, when 'Confidential Information' was previously defined and subsequently replaced with 'Protected Information', introduces another layer of inconsistency. It's unclear if 'Confidential Data' is distinct from both 'Confidential Information' and 'Protected Information,' further muddying the scope of the exceptions to the non-disclosure obligations. The meaning of Confidential Data remains undefined, even though Confidential Information was defined in section 1 and Protected information replaced it in section 2.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Exceptions. Notwithstanding the provisions of this Agreement, the obligations and restrictions set forth herein regarding Confidential Data shall not apply to information that the Receiving Party can establish (i) is or becomes publicly available other than as a result of a breach of this Agreement by the Receiving Party, (ii) is lawfully received from a third party which is, to the reasonable knowledge and belief of the Receiving Party, not under any obligation of confidentiality for the benefit of the Disclosing Party, (iii) was either in the possession of or known to the Receiving Party at the time of disclosure without any limitation on use or disclosure for the benefit of the Receiving Party, or (iv) is independently developed by the Receiving Party without the use, r eference to or benefit of the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Data.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Confidential Information' to 'Confidential Data' in the Changed Text, while the Contradicted Text also uses 'Confidential Data', creates a contradiction. The Perturbation Explanation states that 'Confidential Information' was previously defined and replaced with 'Protected Information'. Thus, the introduction of 'Confidential Data', which remains undefined, creates ambiguity and inconsistency, as it is unclear whether it is distinct from both 'Confidential Information' and 'Protected Information'. This contradicts the expectation of clear and consistent terminology throughout the contract, as 'Confidential Information' was replaced by 'Protected Information', but the original change now uses 'Confidential Data'.

perturbation.original_text: All materials and Confidential Infor mation shall remain the exclusive property of the Disclosing Party. Except as expressly provided herein or under a separate written agreement between the parties that references this Agreement, either party shall not be obligated to grant, convey or transfer to the other any interest, license or other right, or under its Confidential Information or any patent, copyright, trade secret, trademark or other intellectual property right.
perturbation.changed_text: All materials and Sensitive Information shall remain the exclusive property of the Disclosing Party. Except as expressly provided herein or under a separate written agreement between the parties that references this Agreement, either party shall not be obligated to grant, convey or transfer to the other any interest, license or other right, or under its Sensitive Information or any patent, copyright, trade secret, trademark or other intellectual property right.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'Confidential Information' with 'Sensitive Information' without definition creates confusion regarding ownership rights. It raises questions about whether 'Sensitive Information' encompasses the same materials as originally intended under 'Confidential Information,' or if it includes a different set of data. This affects who owns what, creating potential disputes over intellectual property.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Ownership; No License. All materials and Sensitive Information shall remain the exclusive property of the Disclosing Party. Except as expressly provided herein or under a separate written agreement between the parties that references this Agreement, either party shall not be obligated to grant, convey or transfer to the other any interest, license or other right, or under its Sensitive Information or any patent, copyright, trade secret, trademark or other intellectual property right.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Confidential Information' to 'Sensitive Information' introduces ambiguity because the contract doesn't define 'Sensitive Information'. The 'Contradicted Text' relies on 'Sensitive Information' without a clear definition, leading to potential disputes over ownership and rights, as highlighted in the perturbation explanation. This aligns with the 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction' definition, as the interchangeable use of terms without definition leads to conflicting obligations regarding ownership. The changed text causes contradiction within the document.

perturbation.original_text: Upon the earlier of (i) the completion or (ii) the Disclosing Party’s written r equest, the Receiving Party shall promptly return or destroy all Confidential Information disclosed or made available by the Disclosing Party, in any form and including, all Notes, save to the extent that regulation, applicable rule, law or record retention policy requires retention of such material. Upon request, the Receiving Party shall certify in writing that it has returned or destroyed all Confidential Information in accordance with this paragraph 6, disclosed or made available by the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the earlier of (i) the completion or (ii) the Disclosing Party’s written r equest, the Receiving Party shall promptly return or destroy all Private Information disclosed or made available by the Disclosing Party, in any form and including, all Notes, save to the extent that regulation, applicable rule, law or record retention policy requires retention of such material. Upon request, the Receiving Party shall certify in writing that it has returned or destroyed all Private Information in accordance with this paragraph 6, disclosed or made available by the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: Substituting 'Confidential Information' with 'Private Information' in the return of information clause raises questions about the scope of what must be returned or destroyed. Because this term has not been defined, it creates uncertainty regarding the obligations upon termination or request for information return. The term private information remains undefined.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Return of Confidential Information. Upon the earlier of (i) the completion or (ii) the Disclosing Party’s written r equest, the Receiving Party shall promptly return or destroy all Private Information disclosed or made available by the Disclosing Party, in any form and including, all Notes, save to the extent that regulation, applicable rule, law or record retention policy requires retention of such material. Upon request, the Receiving Party shall certify in writing that it has returned or destroyed all Private Information in accordance with this paragraph 6, disclosed or made available by the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.justification: The original text uses 'Confidential Information', which is presumably a defined term within the contract. The changed text replaces this with 'Private Information', which the explanation states is undefined. The contradicted text also uses the undefined term 'Private Information'. This creates a contradiction because the obligation to return or destroy 'Private Information' is unclear as its scope is not defined, thus conflicting with any other clauses that refer to the return or destruction of Confidential Information. This aligns with the 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction' definition, where using multiple terms interchangeably without defining them leads to conflicting obligations.
